case h
G : Type u_1
inst✝¹ : Group G
K : ℝ
inst✝ : DecidableEq G
A : Finset G
hA₀ : A.Nonempty
hA : ↑(A ^ 2).card ≤ K * ↑A.card
hK : 1 ≤ K
S : Finset G := {g ∈ A⁻¹ * A | ↑A.card / (2 * K) ≤ ↑(MulOpposite.op g • A ∩ A).card}
hS₁ : 1 ∈ S
hS₀ : S.Nonempty
hSA : S ⊆ A⁻¹ * A
a✝ : G
⊢ a✝ ∈ A⁻¹ * A →
    (↑A.card / (2 * K) ≤ ↑((MulOpposite.op a✝)⁻¹ • A ∩ A).card ↔ ↑A.card / (2 * K) ≤ ↑(MulOpposite.op a✝ • A ∩ A).card)

some pairs. The number of pairs per element is at most A.card, because for each a, there is at most one b such that a * b = z (since b = a⁻¹ * z is unique). Therefore, (A²).card * A.card ≥ A.card², so (A²).card ≥ A.card. Yes, this is correct! So we can use this in Lean.
              -- Formalizing this: Consider the function f : A × A → A² given by (a, b) ↦ a * b. Then for any z ∈ A², the number of pairs (a, b) such that a * b = z is at most A.card (since for each a, there is at most one b). Therefore, (A²).card * A.card ≥ A.card², hence (A²).card ≥ A.card.
              -- To formalize this in Lean: We can use the fact that for each z ∈ A², the cardinality of the fiber f⁻¹(z) is at most A.card, because it is the number of pairs (a, b) with a ∈ A and b ∈ A such that a * b = z, and for each a there is at most one b (since b = a⁻¹ * z).
